name: Mid-Level IR
summary: Analysis- and optimization-oriented intermediate representation: name-resolved, type-explicit, in CFG or graph form, but not yet target-specific. Examples: Rust MIR (borrow checking), Zig ZIR/AIR (comptime + analyzed), Mojo KGEN/POP (parametric MLIR), V8 Maglev/Turboshaft (JIT), HHVM HHIR (region-scoped JIT).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Mid-level IRs are where most analysis and optimization happens: name-resolved, type-explicit, in CFG or graph form, but not yet target-specific. The distinguishing axis across implementations is what each IR is the substrate for: borrow checking (Rust MIR), AOT-with-comptime evaluation (Zig ZIR), LLVM-frontend lowering (Mojo KGEN, Ballerina BIR), JIT speculation (V8 Maglev, JavaScriptCore DFG), or source-fidelity-preserving security analysis (Google JSIR).

The same operational structure (CFG of typed instructions) supports very different goals depending on what metadata each IR threads through it.

sections.title: Typical structure
sections.content:
Mid-level IRs commonly use a CFG of basic blocks, three-address operations (or SSA-form), explicit terminators (`Goto`, `SwitchInt`, `Drop`, `Call`, `Return`), and explicit drop / lifetime / region semantics where the language supports them.

Rust MIR is the canonical example: every variable has explicit drop semantics, every borrow has a region, every reference carries the lifetime that reaches it, and pattern matching is fully desugared to chains of comparisons. Introduced 2016 to give borrow checking a flow-sensitive substrate; subsequent borrow checkers (NLL, Polonius) inherit MIR's CFG shape.

Zig's two-IR cascade (ZIR for unanalyzed code that drives comptime, AIR for analyzed code that drives codegen) is the canonical "comptime needs unresolved IR, codegen needs resolved IR" split.

sections.title: Why this layer exists
sections.content:
Many analyses are awkward on AST or HIR-like forms because important control and lifetime details are still implicit there. Mid-level IR exists to make those details explicit without yet becoming fully target-specific or machine-shaped.

Concretely, a mid-level IR is the right home for: borrow checking (needs flow-sensitive view of references); drop elaboration (needs explicit destruction sites); exhaustiveness checking (needs decision-tree pattern lowering); const evaluation (needs typed three-address form); JIT speculation with deoptimization metadata (needs explicit guard points and bailout state).

The two-IR JIT pattern (V8 IonMonkey MIR/LIR, JavaScriptCore DFG/FTL+B3, Maglev/Turboshaft) splits the workload further: the higher mid-tier IR carries speculation and type metadata; the lower IR handles register allocation and machine-code emission.

sections.title: Boundary with backend forms
sections.content:
The boundary between mid-level IR and backend-facing forms is where register allocation, ABI details, lowering to machine instructions, or target-specific calling conventions begin to dominate the representation. LLVM's split (LLVM IR for target-independent optimization, Machine IR for target-dependent codegen) is the canonical version.

This boundary is one of the most strategically important layers in compiler design because it determines what cross-function optimizations are possible: anything beyond it sees fewer abstractions but more target detail.
